nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, typically found over open substrate with low reef and remote bommies at depths of around 10–60 m (recorded down to 91 m); juveniles may occur in shallower coastal reef environments. Tends to form small, loose aggregations along current-swept drop-offs.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves, ledges, and overhangs

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton in the wild, including small crustaceans. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, cyclops, calanus, and over time may take high-quality flake or pellet foods. Small portions multiple times per day (3–4 feedings) are generally advised to support a high metabolism. A refugium cultivating live copepods and amphipods is commonly recommended as a supplemental food source.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish of similar size
avoidWith: other male Red-Bar Anthias (in smaller tanks)

summary: A peaceful Indo-Pacific reef planktivore with striking pink-and-red coloration; generally considered reef-safe and best kept as a harem of one male with several females in a well-established system with frequent feedings.
careNotes:
Pseudanthias cooperi is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant individual in a group will commonly transition to male if no male is present. Males display a light pink body with a vertical red bar across the mid-section; females tend to be more orange with a silvery belly and a red tail. Males have been known to be territorial toward other males of the same species and are generally not housed together unless the tank is very large.

Multiple sources recommend keeping a group of one male with several females. LiveAquaria advises 125+ gallons (about 473 L) for a harem and around 70 gallons (about 265 L) for a single specimen, while AquaInfo and WhiteCorals recommend around 500 L for a small group with 800+ L preferred. Averaging across sources gives roughly 490 L for group keeping; the 500 L figure is used here, biased slightly larger because anthias are active mid-water swimmers that benefit from ample open space and a long footprint.

This species has a high metabolism and has been reported to decline quickly if underfed; multiple small feedings per day are generally advised, and a refugium seeding copepods and amphipods is commonly recommended.

Anthias species in general have been documented jumping from open aquaria, so a tight-fitting lid or full cover is generally advised.

Adult size sources vary: LiveAquaria cites about 4 in (~10 cm), FishBase lists 14 cm TL max, AquaInfo lists about 12–14 cm, and WhiteCorals lists 13–14 cm. The 10–14 cm range used here reflects the typical-to-maximum spread across sources.

Reefs.com (a reference cited in the discovery manifest) returned 403 Forbidden during cross-verification; the secondary slot was filled instead by FishBase, AquaInfo, and WhiteCorals.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered difficult and has not been routinely documented in home aquariums. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is absent. Broadcast spawning has been reported in large display systems, but raising the small pelagic larvae on a zooplankton diet is rarely successful outside dedicated breeder setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: tank size (70 gal single / 125 gal group), temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, peaceful temperament, reef-safe.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Family Anthiadidae, Order Perciformes), max adult size 14 cm TL, temperature range 22–27°C, depth 10–91 m (usually 10–60 m), Indo-Pacific distribution from East Africa to Samoa and the Line Islands, southern Japan to the Great Barrier Reef.

additional.name: AquaInfo
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (500 L group / 800+ L preferred), adult size 12–14 cm, temperature 22–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, planktivore diet, protogynous hermaphroditism, reef-safe.

additional.name: WhiteCorals
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank size (500 L), adult size 13–14 cm, temperature 22–27°C, group-keeping recommendation. Used to average minimum tank volume.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary in the Stage A manifest returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on fetch; replaced by FishBase, AquaInfo, and WhiteCorals for cross-verification.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change occurs socially. Captive spawning has been reported in large display systems but larval rearing is rarely successful in home aquariums.
